spanish_cities.name: spanish_cities
spanish_cities.label: Spanish cities
spanish_cities.description:
# Spanish Cities

Cities in Spain with population >= 5,000, sourced from the
[GeoNames](https://www.geonames.org/) `cities5000` dataset
(CC BY 4.0). Sorted by population, capped at 5,000 rows
(the full set is 1,476 at this population threshold).
Dense regional dataset — useful for testing fixed-resolution
hexbin sizing (Region, City, District, Neighborhood).

orders.name: orders
orders.label: Orders
orders.description:
This table has basic information about orders, as well as some derived
facts based on payments

# Order Status

Orders can be one of the following statuses:

| status         | description                                                                                                            |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| placed         | The order has been placed but has not yet left the warehouse                                                           |
| shipped        | The order has ben shipped to the customer and is currently in transit                                                  |
| completed      | The order has been received by the customer                                                                            |
| return_pending | The customer has indicated that they would like to return the order, but it has not yet been received at the warehouse |
| returned       | The order has been returned by the customer and received at the warehouse                                              |

delivery_speed_tier.name: delivery_speed_tier
delivery_speed_tier.label: Delivery Speed Tier
delivery_speed_tier.sql:
CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'

  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

delivery_speed_tier.name: delivery_speed_tier
delivery_speed_tier.label: Delivery Speed Tier
delivery_speed_tier.sql:
CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'

END

orders.label: Orders
orders.description:
This table has basic information about orders, as well as some derived
facts based on payments

# Order Status

Orders can be one of the following statuses:

| status         | description                                                                                                            |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| placed         | The order has been placed but has not yet left the warehouse                                                           |
| shipped        | The order has ben shipped to the customer and is currently in transit                                                  |
| completed      | The order has been received by the customer                                                                            |
| return_pending | The customer has indicated that they would like to return the order, but it has not yet been received at the warehouse |
| returned       | The order has been returned by the customer and received at the warehouse                                              |

delivery_speed_tier.name: delivery_speed_tier
delivery_speed_tier.label: Delivery Speed Tier
delivery_speed_tier.sql:
CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

  WHEN ("visit_analytics".days_since_last_visit) <= 90 THEN 'Regular (31-90 days)'
  WHEN ("visit_analytics".days_since_last_visit) <= 180 THEN 'Infrequent (91-180 days)'
  ELSE 'Rare (>180 days)'
END

customer_order_payments.name: customer_order_payments
customer_order_payments.label: Customer order payments
customer_order_payments.description:
Wide table joining customers, orders, order line items, and payments.
**Compound Fanout**: This table joins two one-to-many relationships through orders: - orders → line_items (1:N) - orders → payments (1:N)
Result: Each order with M line items and N payments produces M × N rows.
Example: Order #9 has 3 line items and 2 payments = 6 rows in this table.
**Deduplication with sum_distinct**: - Order-level metrics (shipping_cost): dedupe by `order_id` - Line item metrics (line_item_total): dedupe by `line_item_id` - Payment metrics (payment_amount): dedupe by `payment_id`

CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'

timezone_test.name: timezone_test
timezone_test.label: Timezone test
timezone_test.description:
Synthetic dataset for testing data timezone (warehouse session timezone) behavior. 31 timestamptz events. When dataTimezone is set, DATE_TRUNC boundaries shift, producing different per-bucket counts.
Rows 1-10: interior rows in Jan 15-16 2024 UTC.
Rows 11-18: boundary rows.
  - 11 tokyo_midnight   2024-01-14 15:00Z  -> Tokyo DAY 2024-01-15
  - 12 utc_midnight     2024-01-15 00:00Z  -> PP DAY 2024-01-14
  - 13 dst_pre_spring   2024-03-10 06:30Z  -> NY HOUR 01:00 EST
  - 14 dst_post_spring  2024-03-10 07:30Z  -> NY HOUR 03:00 EDT (02:00 gap)
  - 15 dst_pre_fall     2024-11-03 05:30Z  -> NY 01:30 EDT
  - 16 dst_post_fall    2024-11-03 06:30Z  -> NY 01:30 EST (fall-back; PG/DuckDB/Redshift same bucket as 15, CH/BQ may split)
  - 17 year_boundary    2024-01-01 00:00Z  -> Tokyo YEAR 2024, PP YEAR 2023
  - 18 month_boundary   2024-01-31 15:00Z  -> Tokyo MONTH 2024-02

Rows 19-31: monthly_dst series — one row per month, Jan 2024 → Jan 2025, placed at noon America/New_York. DATE_TRUNC(month) in NYC produces a continuous monthly axis that crosses both DST transitions (Mar 10 spring forward, Nov 3 fall back). Used to exercise the cartesian category date axis snap for non-UTC DST timezones.

orders.name: orders
orders.label: Orders
orders.description:
This table has basic information about orders, as well as some derived
facts based on payments

# Order Status

Orders can be one of the following statuses:

| status         | description                                                                                                            |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| placed         | The order has been placed but has not yet left the warehouse                                                           |
| shipped        | The order has ben shipped to the customer and is currently in transit                                                  |
| completed      | The order has been received by the customer                                                                            |
| return_pending | The customer has indicated that they would like to return the order, but it has not yet been received at the warehouse |
| returned       | The order has been returned by the customer and received at the warehouse                                              |

delivery_speed_tier.name: delivery_speed_tier
delivery_speed_tier.label: Delivery Speed Tier
delivery_speed_tier.sql:
CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END
delivery_speed_tier.description: Expected number of days for order delivery
delivery_speed_tier.compiledSql:
CASE
  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ("orders".estimated_delivery_days) <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

END

This table has basic information about orders, as well as some derived

# Order Status

Orders can be one of the following statuses:

| status         | description                                                                                                            |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| placed         | The order has been placed but has not yet left the warehouse                                                           |
| shipped        | The order has ben shipped to the customer and is currently in transit                                                  |
| completed      | The order has been received by the customer                                                                            |
| return_pending | The customer has indicated that they would like to return the order, but it has not yet been received at the warehouse |
| returned       | The order has been returned by the customer and received at the warehouse                                              |

delivery_speed_tier.name: delivery_speed_tier
delivery_speed_tier.label: Delivery Speed Tier
delivery_speed_tier.sql:
CASE
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} = 1 THEN 'Same/Next Day'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 3 THEN 'Fast (2-3 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 5 THEN 'Standard (4-5 days)'
  WHEN ${estimated_delivery_days} <= 7 THEN 'Extended (6-7 days)'
  ELSE 'Slow (8+ days)'
END

END

country_orders.name: country_orders
country_orders.label: Country orders
country_orders.description:
Orders by country, designed to illustrate the period-over-period (PoP)
comparison problem when countries appear or disappear between periods.

- India: new country added in Jan 2025, no 2024 data
- Germany: had orders in 2024 but none in 2025

An inner join PoP comparison will silently drop both India and Germany
because they don't exist in both periods. A full outer join or union
of period spines is needed to preserve all countries.

nested_agg_fanout_repro.name: nested_agg_fanout_repro
nested_agg_fanout_repro.label: Repro: Nested agg + fanout
nested_agg_fanout_repro.description: Reproduction metric for nested aggregate + fanout CTE compilation. Example repro: fanouts_accounts explored by fanouts_users.job_title, metric nested_agg_fanout_repro, sorted DESC by the same metric.
nested_agg_fanout_repro.compiledSql:
CASE
  WHEN (SUM("fanouts_accounts".estimated_annual_recurring_revenue)) IS NULL THEN NULL
  ELSE 1 + (
    0.99 * COALESCE(
      100.0 * (
        MAX((SUM("fanouts_accounts".estimated_annual_recurring_revenue))) OVER () - (SUM("fanouts_accounts".estimated_annual_recurring_revenue))
      ) / NULLIF(
        MAX((SUM("fanouts_accounts".estimated_annual_recurring_revenue))) OVER () - MIN((SUM("fanouts_accounts".estimated_annual_recurring_revenue))) OVER (),
        0
      ),
      0
    )
  )
END

fanouts_accounts_bridged_fanout.name: fanouts_accounts_bridged_fanout
fanouts_accounts_bridged_fanout.label: Fanouts accounts bridged fanout
fanouts_accounts_bridged_fanout.description:
This model demonstrates a many-to-many join pattern that creates extreme metric inflation:
Accounts → Deals → User_Deals (junction table) → Users

This creates a complex fanout where:
- Each account can have multiple deals
- Each deal can have multiple users (through the junction table)
- Each user can work on multiple deals (through the junction table)

The result is that account records get multiplied by the total number of user-deal combinations,
creating extreme fanout that significantly inflates COUNT metrics while leaving COUNT_DISTINCT safe.

This demonstrates how many-to-many relationships through junction tables can create 
multiplicative fanout effects that are much more severe than simple 1-to-many joins.
